commentBody:
Francis,
You asked about CAR BOMB late in the Monday comments. See 4/24/2022, 33 Across. Then read the Wordplay comments for that puzzle, perhaps starting with Reader Picks.
500 day gold streak. Previous high was 53. I’d love to say I’ve improved that much, but the late week puzzles have definitely been nerfed over the last couple years.
Nice puzzle.  Great revealer.
Geez-o-Pete, that was a strange clue! Yeesh!
Gonna try to never think about this one again... the theme was cool but what a sloppy puzzle

Brilliant construction Kathy, and hat’s off to Sam for eloquently explaining its brilliance! I missed that the bent truths came from a lie during the fill, but it’s just the more satisfying to note on reflection.

This is a wonderful example of a Tuesday that seems very difficult to construct, but solves as easily as a Tuesday. Usually stunt puzzles come to some expense of the solver, but here, I’m just impressed.

That was a fun puzzle with a cool theme.  I thought it was pretty tough by Tuesday standards.

I picked up pretty early that the circled words were synonyms for truth and liked the BENDS THE TRUTH revealer, but a couple of them were still kind of tough to spot.  Then the COVER STORY and FAKE NAME with identical clues was nice.

The southwest corner in particular I thought was tough.  C SPOTS was something I hadn't heard in a while.  Then OH HEY felt oddly phrased and SHOUT seemed like a very imprecise answer for the clue to the point where I wasn't sure it was right when I put it in.  All crossing OED as an acronym for Oxford English Dictionary which I had to look up after to learn.  Then FOAL where I wanted it to be CALF first and OSSA crossed with ETALIA.

Most of the puzzle felt like a regular Tuesday but that corner had a few clues I felt like big reaches that made it much harder.

Thirty years ago when I was of college age, I convinced my parents that I wanted to pursue a career in cheek quivering. Essentially it’s what Nixon did when he spoke, but to a more refined and controlled degree. The problem was there was only one cheek quivering conservatory in the whole world at the time, a far cry from today, when they seem to be on every corner now, even more plentiful than Starbucks. The conservatory was located in the Himalayas, just at the base of the south col of Mt. Everest, so getting there was a bit of a trek, and, being the only school of its kind, the tuition was exorbitant. $3Million per semester, and it was a seven year program. It would have been even more expensive but I agreed to commute each day from STATEN ISLAND to save my parents some money.  All in all I’d say the program was pretty good, but not great. I feel like if I went back today it would be a more comprehensive syllabus, but the program was in its infancy then. All-in-all I don’t regret going because I made some lifelong friends and partied really hard, but if I had to do it all over again I’d probably have chosen a normal college and a career in something lucrative like Eleanor Roosevelt impersonation, or pogo stick salesman impersonation.
Awesome, really enjoyed this. Loved the final twist!
loving the wordplay in today's puzzle!
Very enjoyable puzzle!  I didn't catch the 'bent truths' until nearly the end, a pleasant surprise!  Thank you for the fun!

First off, a shout-out to Sam. "Kathy Bloomer won’t give us a straight answer" is as brilliant as the puzzle itself. Kudos! My last square to fall was the G of GABLED. First, I had a C. Then, out of nowhere, Jumpin' Jack Flash popped into my mind. "It's a GAS, GAS, GAS" and that's how [rip-roaring good time] resolved the "almost there/keep trying/you're an embarrassment on a Tuesday" banner across the grid. I did think of that song yesterday, courtesy of the triple jump puzzle. But today, there's no way I can not post Blue Bayou... https://youtu.be/Kp9G0zkorio?is=2g080s3HumEjfBEy Thank you, Ms. Bloomer, for a puzzle with a great theme and rich fill that must have been a bear to construct. I'm not BENDing THE TRUTH when I say I'm looking forward to more from you!

A sly Tuesday puzzle, with some clever angles.  With 20A I saw what was going on and looked forward to the next ones.  Great fun.

Thank you, Kathy Bloomer.  I'm still smiling.

Glad to see violists getting some love, with not only the  ALTO clef mentioned today (which usually has been clued differently), but also with the CCLEF a couple of weeks ago. Didn’t think I’d be thinking about that clef so frequently!
I liked the paired clues/answers at 36A and 53D — Hide NOR hair and Hyde and HARE.

I’m starting to believe there’s a socialist plot to create cruciverbal justice and equality in the NYT, because while late puzzles may be getting a bit easier it also seems like the early week puzzles are getting more challenging (no complaints from me, anyone else have a say?)  I did not get very far at all with acrosses on this one which is a very unusual Tuesday for me. 

Also there’s a decent amount of excellent theme material especially with two crossing the same entry. And bonus entries of TAXSCANDAL and PHONESCAMS in a puzzle about bending the truth? Deliberate or lucky? Chef’s kiss there.

I also think there’s a plot to kidnap constructors before they can write their notes lately, which I miss, because I’d like to hear Kathy’s story about this one. I would include a meme from “A Few Good Men” but suffice it to say that Ms. Bloom is certainly not one who “can’t handle the truth”.

I'm always happy to see a Tuesday dressed up, puffed up and strutting its stuff like a Thursday.

An unexpectedly chewy start to my early-week morning.

Thanks, Kathy and editors.

Felt more like a Wednesday than a Tuesday with a Thursday twist in the theme.

More than I wanted but fun none the less

Like it or not, I'm going to tell you everything I know about the Hale-Bopp comet in the next paragraph.

A cult got associated with it.

I am a very humbled space enthusiast, to know nothing other than human frailty has eclipsed a wonderful celestial object.

I promise I'll pay more attention to it next time it comes around.

I cannot BENDTHETRUTH, that was a terrific puzzle!! It's fun when I figure out the theme early on and can use it to help fill in some unknowns.

At 11 down I had seXSCANDALS, and just moved on without checking my crosses. A crossword sin, no doubt, but I guess better than either a sex or a tax scandal! Anyhow, that's why I didn't get the music but I figured it out pretty quickly because cross is obviously make no sense. 

Fun, fun!
Great puzzle, but calling C-spot slang for a $100 is just objectively false in that slang is used in everyday speech. Give the under 30s a chance!! Maybe clue it as retro slang?

I (almost) solved it without using any of the Down clues in 39 minutes, only the Acrosses. Almost “yay me”. I got absolutely stuck on CSPOTS and OSSA, was not sure of either of them, and eventually gave up. Turns out i had them both right but had misspelled IDINA… Argh!

Longest Tuesday time ever. And I couldn’t be more pleased.

Viva tough Tuesdays!
I liked this and thought it was quite difficult! Is it just me who could not parse "deices" as "de-ices"?? I was reading it like a Spanish word and it was the last section of the puzzle I got!

A tiny bit of Latin nitpickery.  Et Alia implies people.  Et Alia = and others (people), Et Cetera = and other things.

Fun puzzle though, and I enjoyed the twist at the end.

Proof that a puzzle can be (relatively) easy, yet have elegant construction. Thank you, Ms. Bloomer!

(Notes on the use of musical clefs after work.)

This was fun. 

Never having made a crossword, I would think that creating a puzzle easy enough for early in the week, yet tricky enough to be fun, would be a challenge. This one managed both. I didn't see the truth benders until the end, but clever idea.

I've never heard of a hundred-dollar bill being called a CSPOT, but once it became clear that CnOTe wasn't going to work I didn't have much choice. On the other hand, I do remember hearing people in my grandparents' generation (born probably in the 1880's or 1890's, referring to margarine as OLEO. Nobody younger than that, though.

Now, out of curiosity, does anyone know when they--whoever "they" are--decided to drop the possessives in comet names? The first time I recall hearing it was when what I would call Kohoutek's Comet was being called the Comet Kohoutek.
Good Tuesday puzzle, enjoyed the shenanigans of 50A!

Oh, the magnitude of what Kathy pulled off today!  I'll try to explain...

When you have answers that bend in right angles, as the circled words do, it greatly hampers the word possibilities that can go around them, because it constricts the possibilities in two directions, rather than the usual one.  It is challenge enough to create a grid in which this happens five times, as today’s does.

But then, Kathy also had the right-angled answers coming out of other answers – all long! – an addition of complexity which makes building this grid seemingly impossible.  And she pulls it off!    

PLUS, she had to find four examples of truth-bending in which the beginning of a synonym for “truth” is embedded, such as the first five letters of HONESTY nested in PHONE SCAMS.  

What?  Really??  Wow!!!

I bow down to you today, Kathy.  This puzzle is a triumph, the work of a master.  And it was fun to solve for me because the theme’s layers unfolded in stages.  Thank you – this was stunning!

Note on 58A:
Most common commercial oleos (or margarines) contain a small amount of milk products. So if you're trying to be rigidly vegan, or have a VERY severe dairy allergy, best to read the ingredients list.

Haven't seen 58A. in a while!

Very nice construction, just the right amount of head scratching!

Interesting to see 2D [Tallest animal in North America] MOOSE in the Mini today and 

32D [Most dangerous animal in Maine, it's said] MOOSE in the Crossword today.

That makes two MOOSES, I guess.

How beautiful to see such a complex theme in a Tuesday puzzle.  
It's clear that the constructor had her work cut out for her to pull off the trick(s) she had in mind.  Such a lovely and amusing combination of T/RUTH and Fiction.
But often, the constructor's cleverness results in us solvers simply saying, "Yeah, I see what you did.  Very nice, but it didn't improve the pleasure of my solve."
Not so today.
We can enjoy the constructor's cleverness and we can enjoy a lovely solving experience.
And on a Tuesday!

Hats off to you, Kathy Bloomer!

Seemed like a solid enough Tuesday puzzle. Took me almost twelve minutes, which is close to average for me.

Assuming that COVERSTORY, FAKENAME, PHONESCAMS and TAXSCANDAL were also thematic, this theme occupied a lot of the grid. Still, the fill was okay, given the constraints imposed by the expansive theme.

One nit: To me, CLARITY comes awfully close to being an "unbent" truth.

Wonder what Aesop might have done with the MOOSE and the HARE. Then again, no moose in Greece.
Hands up if you had another SCANDAL in mind (that also ends in X) before you corrected to TAX SCANDAL.

[Huff and Puff] might be a good ‘truth in advertising’ name for a line of skinny jeans aimed at the too-old-to-be-wearing-them crowd. It’s a truth you can squeeze and twist out of shape, but then you can’t bend it.

Nice Tuesday puzzle.

Nice Tuesday puzzle. Bit of a workout for me, of course, but catching on to the theme was a big help and that's always a nice touch. And of course a puzzle find today. A Sunday from September 16, 2018 by Joel Fagliano with the title "Uh, What?" Some theme answers: OREGONTRANSPLANT TURNTHECORONER KINGJAMESBUYABLE RIOTINGONTHEWALL PROJECTRUNAWAY SENATOROFGRAVITY Very much in the vicinity of dad jokes. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/16/2018&h=27a ....
Very satisfying Tuesday. Thoroughly enjoyed, thanks!
I really struggled with this one, for a Tuesday! The lower left side was particularly difficult -
So very clever for a Tuesday. I loved it. Like a Thursday without the brain pain.
I just had to jump back on here to marvel at this amazing puzzle. What a great theme! And how artful the fill, considering how branch-like the theme entries were. Loved it.

Still waiting to hear a rebuttle from CLM or CQ, on behalf of the *real* most dangerous animals in Maine…

(and I’m not talking hiding cats nor hairy quahogs :)

A nicely Toughened Up Tuesday puzzle from Kathy. Chuffed that I recalled the HALE BOPP, no relation to Baby BOP. (miss you, Sue Jean) The theme had me singing this one from Mr. Joel. https://youtu.be/MLkUn_8Wqlo?si=nNjihE2dtppFA1R2

Note for SP:
Following NYT style, our columnist refers to the constructor as Ms. Bloomer. From her 2018 debut: "When I retired from my Seattle pediatric hospitalist job in 2014, I became interested in crossword construction."

Well, this was a very nice Tuesday puzzle!  --the kind that favors a person who habitually begins at the bottom of a puzzle!  (Ain't that the TRUTH?!)  

I had forgotten Hale-BOPP, which is fun to say.  At first I had in mind BYRD, but Oops! That's a neighbor of ours.  Silly me.
I was tempted by the --TS at the end of 26A to put GRANT on the $100-bill, but it would have hurt Ben Franklin's feelings.

We recently identified the C CLEF for viola, and today it's ALTO... PhysDau is never around when I need her these days! Ah, well.  Superannuated Parents...what good are we after "the kids" reach their Forties?  

Well, we had another day of Sun-STORM-Sun, followed by a night-time broken by a 2-hour thunderstorm with sideways rain.  Flooding, sinkholes, trees down, power out, roads and parks closed..... and in other news,  Europe in desperate straits with the heat domes making things miserable...   Need to appease the Weather Gods!
I liked the puzzle and all that, but NOES? Come on man.

What incredible density of theme in this puzzle!  To get all these truthy words to BEND in the grid and and at the same time to have all these UNtruthy activities in the grid as well: COVER STORY; TAX SCANDAL; FAKE NAME; and PHONE SCAMS  -- an amazing piece of construction!  Kudos, Kathy!

And thanks to a puzzle earlier in the month, I have mended my decades-long ignoring-of-tiny-little-circles-that-I-don't need-to-figure-out-in-order-to-solve-the-puzzle ways.  I call it the birth of my newborn OLD MAN AND THE SEA curiosity.  While I still don't look at tiny little circles while solving, if a revealer piques my curiosity, I'll go back and look at what the constructor has wrought.  And in this case, as in that earlier one, it was well-worth checking out.  A very clever and original puzzle.

This puzzle was excellent; just the right amount of confounding and a very fun theme. My only gripe is the tricky clues section of the article: in my eyes, those were some of the easiest solves! Difficulty is in the eye of the beholder, but man, did it smart to see none of my unfilled answers there '^^

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle
a/ eye in a lie greeting sounds signal sign in-out-in hide the tidy wipe with-only-with a little neural empire and o to lie in ancient oil the hair the little viola in a blue glade of false outrage d/ a rip-roaring good time in transparent frenzy to watch the way one might step on a stray a/ oh hey okay okay
Lovely and just tricky enough puzzle. I remember squeezing the oleo bag as a child when butter was too rich for the family budget.

This is my slowest Tuesday yet! I got weirdly tangled in the left with HALEBOPP being close to HONESTY - I thought that the Y was a lead off from HALLEYS comet.

I realize this makes zero sense but I also couldn't parse the clues in that area either. 

Oh well haha - it was a fun and clever puzzle!

C notes is a slang term for 100 dollar bills.
C spots is not. 
Give me a break!
I’ll be honest, this was truly a great puzzle. And you can take that to the bank!
I think that was the first time that the revealer was the very last thing I filled, and only then did I get the gimmick! Twelve stars, best Tuesday ever.

I was driving through Montreal on the way to La Ronde, and there was a stop sign, partially covered by a tree branch. My passenger spotted it, and called out, "ARRET, ARRET!" I replied, "A what?" and drove right through the intersection.

I honestly didn't care for this puzzle. It's a little too "oh, aren't I clever" for a Tuesday submission. I'm sure the constructor had to make compromises for the theme to work. Unfortunately, some of them, like NOES and CSPOT, just took me right out of the mode. I quit caring and used the cheater tools when I was less than halfway through.
Far more difficult than your typical Tuesday. Should have saved until tomorrow.

Anyone else notice that STEEPLES is covered by HONESTY?

Just me?  Okay, then.  

I'll just see my way to the inferno.

Oh, MY!

Never thought I would see C-SPOT in a puzzle. 

I would imagine this was the only way its cluing would pass muster with the censors. 

(Yes, C-SPOT is a legit, if less common, slang term for a Benjamin... even if you, personally, have never heard it)

Delightful today. 
Yes, we are gripped by unusual heat right now. Last night a huge thunderstorm, although not as severe as some here have described.
That's the toughest Tuesday I've done in a long time.

What a genius piece of construction! That one is going on my Griddies list! I’m with Lewis on being flabbergasted by Ms. Bloomer’s constructing skills. I’m sad we didn’t get any Constructor’s Comments though. I would like to know how long it took to create. I thought this one was nearly lost in the bottom third, but switched to Light Mode and the circles helped me figure out the revealer. I’m hoping I can keep the Tuesday streak going!

I figured out a work around for those who want to access the column in the NYT app instead of the games app. After opening it on the games app, click on the writer for the column and it will open their page on the NYT app, then simply click on “Latest”  and select the most recent crossword column. Voila!

So, a couple days ago we had C CLEF, and today we have ALTO clef, both clued in regards to violas.

There are three clefs: F-, C-, and G-clefs. They were origianlly fancy versions of those letters, and they mark  on which line of those pitches fall.

Put C-clef is on the middle line of the staff, and it's called the "alto clef," used in viola parts, in the lower part of its range; put it on the second line from the top, and it's called the "tenor clef," used by cellos, bassoons, and trombones, in the *upper* part of their ranges.
 
BUT, there is also the soprano clef (the C-clef on the lowest line of the staff), and the mezzo-soprano clef (C-clef on the second from bottom0, and even the baritone clef (C-clef on the top line of the staff, or the F-clef on the middle--it works out to the same.) And these were all previously used for vocal music, through the end of the 19th c.--you will see them in Dover reprints of older editions of Bach and Wagner.

And not just in vocal music: Bach, et alii, would use the soprano clef for the right hand parts of keyboard music, and even Brahms sometimes used the alto clef in the left hand parts of organ music. And there was something called the "French violin clef"--G-clef on the bottom line--I guess because French violinists played higher than Italian ones? 

Modern tenors (singers) usually use the G-clef, read down an octave--sometimes there is a little "8" put below it to distinguish it.
Needed real flexibility for this one. Many thanks.

I have mixed feelings on this puzzle. First, I agree with many that this was placed on the WRONG day… However, I LOVED the theme and how it helped fill those circles… Then, the obscure wording comes in… Now I have NEVER claimed to have the best vocabulary, but it isn’t bad either and I have never heard of DEICES the road especially with some tricky crosses. 

If this would have been published on a Wednesday- Saturday it would have been more appropriate, so I am not hating on the constructor.

Rating:
8/10
NOES?!?!?! Crossing OSSA, which crosses ETALIA? Not good.
Very clever puzzle but felt more like a Wednesday.  I think more people would be happy worders in that scenario.  But I did enjoy learning about gabled roofs
Really brilliant construction, Kathy, as well as wonderfully clever commentary by Sam! This was a longer than usual Tuesday solve for me, but was so enjoyable!😊
Very clever and fun puzzle! And I don't know what everyone is complaining about - I actually finished 41 seconds under my average Tuesday time!
Oof. Upper right and lower left had me stumped.
Only gripe is that all of the clues with bends solved to a real phrase except the revealer.
Some uneven cluing leading to some dry spell areas, as others seem to have also experienced. Would have liked to see it more even overall, but enjoyed the challenge and the theme was cute.

This went quickly for me but I really enjoyed it.

When I got to the revealer on my first pass I had enough of the middle-east filled in to see CANDOR, and I confidently entered BENDSTruth at 50A, with a little grumbling that I'd never heard the phrase without the word "the".  When I got around to solving the rest of the SE corner it was very satisfying to realize that the THE was right where it was supposed to be.
You've got to be kidding me. She won't give us a Tuesday puzzle either. Did I sleep through a few days? Miss for me.
Very fun and very clever.

OLEO must be a regional thing.  Margarine was just margarine when i was growing up.  Cross that with an obscure HALLEYBOPP, and the section became a slog for me.

I'll echo what everyone else said about NOES.

The puzzle was clever, but the execution could use some polishing.

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from September 6, 1992 by Albert J. Klaus with the title: "Zoological links." Four 21 letter grid-spanning theme answers in that one. Don't recall another quite like this. Here are those clues and answers: "COW__" BELLBOYSCOUTMASTERKEY "DOG__" TAGTEAMWORKSHOPKEEPER "CAT__ " TAILGATECRASHLANDLORD "HORSE__" HAIRPINWHEELCHAIRLIFT Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/6/1992&h=54a ....
On what planet was today’s puzzle a Tuesday?
The puzzles aren’t great these days. Why am I still paying for this subscription?
I don’t generally comment on Monday and Tuesday puzzles, but I thought I’d pop in to say that I found this puzzle charming and with a bit more grit than typical of Tuesdays.
As someone born in 1995, I fear I had no chance with answers like CSPOT, OLEO and HALEBOPP. Better luck next Tuesday.
I see that many found this difficult. I was 25 percent faster than my Tuesday average ¯\_(ツ)_/¯ Sometimes a puzzle clicks and sometimes it doesn't. Snarky comments won't change that.
Thought this puzzle was cute, but then loved it on a whole new level after Sam pointed out the bent truths all stem from lies - brilliant!
Absolutely loved this one - had a giggle once I got the theme.

This was lots of fun. Thank you Kathy Bloomer for giving us all something to chuckle about. It took me a bit of time once I'd finished to see the whole concept, and Sam's explanation was certainly a help.

Too many intersecting proper nouns/foreign language answers. 

Not how you make a good puzzle.
This was NOT a Tuesday puzzle. Coulda been a Wednesday or an easy Friday. Annoyed.

Rough Tuesday. Unnecessarily difficult for the day. For those in the comments talking about how good they are and how they didn’t have a problem, great, how quickly we forget what it’s like to be new. I would wager when you started doing this you probably started with Mondays and Tuesday’s to get better and grow. The whole point of easier days is to allow more people to participate and get better.

Puzzles like this ruin the whole concept. Congrats on getting it. So did I, but making it this challenging on this day prevents others that may be newer from growing and getting better and participating.

There are decades and decades of Thursday, Friday, Saturday and Sundays that may be more your speed if having slightly easier days is so annoying to you.

This puzzle would make for a great Wednesday.  But it is not a good Tuesday. No fault to the creator who made a good puzzle, this is a NYT issue. They put the puzzle on the wrong day of the week.

What a phenomenal puzzle!!!!
It had that Dayenu quality (it would’ve been enough to have a clever theme, Dayenu… have a clever theme and have five, Dayenu.  but then the longer ones were false things!
Oh my god!!!!!

(Bugs me that NYT style is to ignore the tile of “Dr.”
Manny Nosowsky’s obituary was “Mr Nosowsky this …Mr nosowsky that…” He had been addressed as Dr Nosowsky for over 70 years!  Who is the NYT to demote him! ;))

That said, fabulous puzzle, Dr Bloomer!
This puzzle was exhausting. Keep early week puzzles accesible not a bragging excuse for the wise know-it-alls!
The Incan civilization was not "ancient". It existed during the 15th and 16th centuries AD.

@Barry Ancona

Wow...can't recall ever seeing a Wordplay commentariat poison-pen dossier quite like that one.

@Barry Ancona

Thanks, Barry, I appreciate it very much.
I've never heard it, either, and would like to know what is meant by it.

@Wyra,

TIL nerfed. Thank you!

@Wesley,

Don’t sell yourself short. A 500-day streak is nothing to sneeze at. Congratulations!!

@Ryan

I respect your opinion, but I’d love to hear why you think so because it seems like a pretty good grid and clues to me.

@SP

Normally I would jump in here with something snarky, but I'm going to try to pretend to act like I'm good.

@Ryan
I don't get your point.  
Nothing has extended into the margins (which has happened occasionally!)  There is no "alternate spelling" to sully the symmetry or force a rhyme.  No jangling adjectives or dangling modifiers.  Perhaps you should gracefully withdraw your objection?
Ave Atque Vale

@Francis

Yea, Matt from Maine (who may be the most dangerous animal in Maine, above the MOOSE) has reminded me to show more restraint. Still, I can see early week solvers getting annoyed at the difficulty level of this, but this was about as unsloppy a grid as I can imagine so would love to hear what is on Ryan’s mind.

@Chris
remember the OED; it'll be back.

@Ace:
Haha, your mini essays remind me of a commenter we used to have here, from Waterloo Ontario. Like you, he had perfected the art of the COVER STORY.  He had another habit, of ending his tale in the

@Ace

I love a well-spun tall tale.

Once I delighted everyone at the Algonquin Table with a hilarious crack about Dorothy Parker.

She laughed and I laughed, and we drank, and we put on the gloves and she broke my nose.

@sotto voce

Only Linda Ronstadt could leap over the bar that was set so high by Roy Orbison.

@sotto voce

Once again thank you for your musical contribution, exceeded only by your cogent, amusing and gentle comments. They don't make 'em like Linda anymore (and I will never get over the roller skate hot pants)

@Mtmetz

Aw, that's so sweet of you. Thank you!

Roller skate hot pants? I see her in jeans in the video, so I apologize that your comment went over my head.
 
In any case, I wouldn't have been checking out hot pants; the very short men's soccer shorts of the 80's and 90's are more up my alley. 
In fact, I love the World Cup, but it's a little less interesting nowadays. 😂

@sotto voce

Amen, And the slide in her guitar.

@Steve

I knew that if I asked this learned assembly a question I could google, I'd get responses as interesting as this.

Thanks a million!  

No, make it a billion!
@Francis asking questions to humans like shopping in shops is a great experience. The online world has its uses but the side effects are not necessarily good. We are a social species. 😀

@Susan Hoffman
In an already challenging and fun puzzle, those paired clues were the cherries atop this Tuesday sundae.

@SP

Wow and I didn’t  even mention FAKENAME and COVERSTORY. This is looking more and more like an early week POY contender all the time.

@SP

I hadn't caught it, but Sam pointed out how those four "untruths" were intentional, hiding the "truths."
Wow, right?

I'm with you on early-week  POY contender.

@SP

And PHONESCAMS.

@SP
Your first paragraph summed up my experience,too.

@SP

My apologies, Ms. Bloomer not Bloom

@Matt

Oh, man... It seems to be blocking my efforts to send you fodder of a Freudian sort. In short, all I'm saying is what is a puzzle if it doesn't give me Freudian butter!? 

Potter fodder! 

There we go! Good job voice to text! 

I mean as some old old Patriot sort said, give me an f word that rhymes with codder or give me a d word that rhymes with... Beth.

Yeah, baby!!! Huzzah!!

@HeathieJ

MINNESOTAWHAT?

@HeathieJ,

I’ll have whatever you’re having.
@Francis lots of crossword puzzles to solve until Hale-Bopp come back to our neighbourhood

@Ιασων

😃  Gosh, I knew I was joking, but I didn't know who much I was joking.  

Now I can say this about Hale-Bopp:

1) a cult associated itself with it.
2) its orbital period is about 2400 years.

@Francis 
Showing my age, my first guess was KOHOUTEK - same number of letters, in fact.

@Francis

Good one. I don't think I'll be adding that to my Outlook calendar.

@HeathieJ

In your defense, I guess sex scandals could involve false accounts. “That wasn’t a kiss! It was vertical CPR!” “That’s not a hotel receipt. The Motel 6 has an excellent lunch buffet!”

And really, is Rover going to obey, regardless of what you say to him? I’ve met Rover. He’s nothing but trouble.

Just trying to help.

@HeathieJ

As sins go, I'm for lust before greed, and so seX before TAX.
@Stewkak Yep. Google C Spot and $100 bill is no where to be found. A woman's anatomy however...

@DirtyCashDylan

Google “c spot slang” and you do get explanations. 

In Crossworld, the answer isn’t always the first definition. If one meaning dominates, you will have to dig deep in Google to find the secondary meaning. 

The fact that the money meaning is somewhat dated contributes to the confusion.

@Steve L I am. https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/et-al It's more about common usage though. You typically see it in academic or legal contexts to shorten a list of names. Usually if we're talking about things, it's "etc." This is not worth a fight though.

@TV--
Sorry, the clue is deliciously correct--"alia" is the neuter plural of "alium," "other things"; for people, it's "alii" (for men, or mixed groups), or "aliæ" (for women).  Of course in a gendered language like Latin, you would use "alii" and "aliæ" for groups of inanimate, yet grammatically gendered nouns.

@Bill Note on the "French violin clef"--in 1725, François Couperin wrote a piece of programmatic music titled "The Apotheosis of Lully"--in it, Apollo leads the French composer, Jean-Baptiste Lully, to the top of Mt. Parnassus, and introduces him to the Italian composer, Arcangelo Corelli--who is already there--and tells the two to play nicey-nicey with one another. In the score. Couperin depicts Corelli's part through the use of the standard G-clef, Lully's with the French one: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BwO-nO9dvk4&list=RDBwO-nO9dvk4&start_radio=1 (at 18:40, if my time stamp didn't work right.)
